caption: Step 1: Open Overpass Turbo and load the map + query editor workspace
description: Open Overpass Turbo and load the map + query editor workspace
narration: Opening shot. Overpass Turbo gives us a map, an editor, and a city-sized question.
fallback_narration: Opening shot: Overpass Turbo, map ready, editor waiting.

caption: Step 2: Split view: query editor on left, map + controls on right
description: Split view: query editor on left, map + controls on right
narration: This framing helps immediately. Logic on one side, evidence on the other.

caption: Step 3: Write a Hyderabad hospitals query in Overpass QL using a fixed city bounding box
description: Write a Hyderabad hospitals query in Overpass QL using a fixed city bounding box
narration: Our first question is simple. Show us hospitals across Hyderabad, and keep the search area explicit.
fallback_narration: First question for the city: show us the hospitals in Hyderabad.

caption: Step 4: Run query to load all mapped hospitals as explorable markers
description: Run query to load all mapped hospitals as explorable markers
narration: Now we run it. This is the part where the city answers back.
fallback_narration: And... run. Now we let the map do its detective work.

caption: Step 5: Use 'zoom to data' to jump map viewport directly onto Hyderabad results
description: Use 'zoom to data' to jump map viewport directly onto Hyderabad results
narration: Instead of hunting around the map, we jump straight to where the results live.
fallback_narration: There we go, zoom straight to the results.

caption: Step 6: Click map markers to inspect feature tags like name, address, and amenity type
description: Click map markers to inspect feature tags like name, address, and amenity type
narration: A marker turns the dots into actual places, with names, tags, and context.
fallback_narration: Let's tap a marker and see the story behind the pin.

caption: Step 7: Open Data tab to inspect raw tabular output and OSM attributes
description: Open Data tab to inspect raw tabular output and OSM attributes
narration: Data view is the reality check. The map looks nice, but the table tells the truth.
fallback_narration: Quick detour into Data view... this is the raw evidence.

caption: Step 8: Switch back to Map view to visually compare coverage across neighborhoods
description: Switch back to Map view to visually compare coverage across neighborhoods
narration: Then we go back to the map, because spatial patterns are easier to feel than to read.
fallback_narration: Back to the map. Easier to read.

caption: Step 9: Replace with EV charging station query to analyze clean-mobility readiness
description: Replace with EV charging station query to analyze clean-mobility readiness
narration: Same workflow, new question. This time we ask about charging stations instead of hospitals.
fallback_narration: Round two: same rhythm, new question... where are the charging stations?

caption: Step 10: EV charging points rendered; now compare spread versus healthcare locations
description: EV charging points rendered; now compare spread versus healthcare locations
narration: Now the comparison gets interesting. Different services, same city, one visual frame.
fallback_narration: Results are coming in. A little pause here while the map connects the dots.

caption: Step 11: Run school query to map education infrastructure in the same city boundary
description: Run school query to map education infrastructure in the same city boundary
narration: Next layer: schools. The story gets better when we ask the city more than one question.
fallback_narration: Next scene: schools. Same city, new layer of the story.

caption: Step 12: Add pharmacy query as a fourth dataset to build a richer city-services comparison
description: Add pharmacy query as a fourth dataset to build a richer city-services comparison
narration: Now pharmacies join in, and the map starts feeling less like a query result and more like a city portrait.
fallback_narration: Now pharmacies join the cast, and the city starts to feel complete.

caption: Step 13: Single editor workflow lets you iterate quickly across multiple urban datasets
description: Single editor workflow lets you iterate quickly across multiple urban datasets
narration: This is the quiet win. One editor lets us iterate across datasets without changing tools or losing context.
fallback_narration: One editor, many datasets... that is the part I really like.

caption: Step 14: Open Export options for downstream GIS and analytics workflows
description: Open Export options for downstream GIS and analytics workflows
narration: Once the map makes sense, export matters. A good demo should hand off cleanly to the next workflow.
fallback_narration: Open export. Now this can travel.

caption: Step 15: Export supports GeoJSON, KML, GPX, and raw OSM for different tools
description: Export supports GeoJSON, KML, GPX, and raw OSM for different tools
narration: GeoJSON is the practical handoff here. Easy to carry into GIS tools, code, or a web map.

caption: Step 16: Tutorial complete: from iterative query editing to exportable, reproducible geo-data
description: Tutorial complete: from iterative query editing to exportable, reproducible geo-data
narration: Final shot. We went from query editing to clean, exportable geo-data.
